name: concise-research-index
description: Index documents for multi-document research should be 100-150 lines max, focusing on findings summary, grades/assessments, and actionable recommendations rather than duplicating technical details already present in referenced documents.

content: Research documentation structure: create comprehensive single-topic docs (e.g., 06-User-Through-Agent-Collaboration.md) with frontmatter (title, task, status, date), then aggregate into index with quick reference tables, architecture diagrams, performance/cost comparisons. Index must include use-case mapping, service mapping, and pattern mapping for quick lookup.

description: ClawCore->Chimera rename caused merge conflicts that lost GSI2 schema updates in 3 docs and CDK code
resolution: Applied missing GSI2 updates manually. Suggests need for automated schema validation between docs/architecture/*.md and infra/lib/data-stack.ts

name: cascade-doc-updates-schema-change
description: When DataStack moved from single platformTable to 6-table design, 4 downstream sections in Platform-IaC doc required updates (PlatformRuntimeStack interface, TenantAgent interface, main app instantiation, backup resources). Systematic approach: (1) grep for old references, (2) update interfaces first, (3) update implementations, (4) update examples.

content: Development workflow documentation structure: CLAUDE.md covers Overstory conventions, git workflows (worktree isolation, rebase-before-merge, merge order), Seeds issue tracking, Mulch expertise management, CDK patterns, testing gates, and named failure modes.

name: roadmap-with-dependency-graph
description: ROADMAP.md documents 8-phase implementation plan with explicit phase dependencies, completed work tracking, and research corpus summary. Includes Seeds issue IDs for each phase.

name: operational-runbook-condensation
description: Transform comprehensive research docs into ops-team runbooks by extracting action-oriented content (commands, tables, checklists), removing architectural narrative, and organizing by operational phase (deployment, incident, capacity). Quick-reference format optimizes for on-call engineers under pressure.

name: developer-guide-consolidation
description: Transform 30k+ lines of research docs into 500-line practical guide by focusing on WHAT (components, flow, structure) over WHY (decisions, rationale). Structure: overview → architecture → components → packages → data model → workflow → patterns. Include concrete code examples and request flow traces with latency breakdown.

name: dev-guide-consolidation
description: Consolidating 84 research docs into 2 developer guides (quickstart + skills): (1) Identify key user journeys (hello-world → deployment, skill authoring), (2) Extract code examples from research (Strands @tool, CDK stacks, SKILL.md v2), (3) Bridge theory to practice (architecture concepts → working code), (4) Add troubleshooting sections for common pain points (Bedrock access, CDK bootstrap, Slack events)

name: ops-guide-structure
description: Developer guides distill research into actionable procedures with 7 sections: Prerequisites (tools/quotas), Environment Setup (config/bootstrap), Stack Deployment (order/commands), Verification (health checks/tests), Pipeline Setup (CI/CD), Monitoring (dashboards/alarms), Troubleshooting (common issues/fixes). Include concrete commands, timelines, and cost estimates.

content: Operations guides consolidate deep architectural research (80+ docs, 1000+ lines) into focused operator procedures. Target specific personas (platform operators vs tenant admins), provide step-by-step commands with expected output, include troubleshooting sections with cause/fix pairs, and cross-reference deeper architectural docs for context.

aws-sdk-agent-capabilities: AWS Account Agent research series pattern: Separate 'infrastructure services Chimera uses' (Bedrock, KMS) from 'services agents control' (Lambda, EC2, S3). Infrastructure audit covers platform capabilities; agent tools research covers AWS APIs exposed as first-class agent tools. Index document (00-*) provides roadmap for multi-doc series.

AWS Well-Architected Framework as agent decision vocabulary: Six pillars (Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance, Cost, Sustainability) provide structured reasoning framework for infrastructure decisions. Agents evaluate changes against all six pillars and present trade-offs to users (e.g., 'increasing capacity improves Reliability but reduces Cost Optimization'). Chimera already implements all pillars — integration makes principles explicit for agent-driven decision-making.

infrastructure-as-capability-research-pattern: Agent-driven AWS infrastructure provisioning research covers three key areas: (1) CodeCommit as agent workspace with Git-based IaC version control and audit trails, (2) CodePipeline canary orchestration using Step Functions for progressive rollout (5%→25%→50%→100%) with auto-rollback, (3) Cedar PBAC with three authorization tiers (auto-apply for low-risk, PR for medium-risk, blocked for dangerous ops). Real-world examples: video pipeline, data lake cataloging, search scaling, ML experiment infrastructure.

infrastructure-as-capability research pattern: Three-doc structure (CDK generation mechanics, use case catalog, Cedar policy boundaries) provides comprehensive coverage of agent-provisioned infrastructure. Template: (1) HOW (technical implementation), (2) WHY (business value via examples), (3) GUARDRAILS (authorization policies).

name: activity-logging-research-series
description: Created 7-document research series on structured activity logging (182KB): 00-Index (series overview), 01-Architecture (system design), 02-Decision-Logs (reasoning capture), 03-Action-Audit-Trail (beyond CloudTrail), 04-Auto-Generated-ADRs (decision records), 05-Runbook-Auto-Generation (operational docs), 06-Real-Time-Dashboards (live visibility). Documents follow research pattern: YAML-like frontmatter, table of contents, executive summary, deep technical sections, code examples, key takeaways. Series demonstrates documenting conceptual features (not yet implemented) with concrete schemas, query patterns, and implementation examples.

content: research-doc-structure: Research documents in docs/research/ follow structured pattern: (1) Rich frontmatter with title/date/status/tags, (2) Executive summary with key insights, (3) Detailed sections with code examples, (4) Real-world scenarios showing patterns in practice, (5) AWS-specific implementation details, (6) Cross-references to related docs, (7) Key takeaways + sources. Documents balance theoretical frameworks with practical AWS implementations.

name: research-documentation-structure
description: Research docs follow consistent pattern: frontmatter (title, task, status, date), comprehensive sections with code examples, AWS service integration details, comparison tables, and cross-references. Index files catalog completed documents and outline planned work.

name: continuous-self-improvement-doc-synthesis
description: Comprehensive research document (07-continuous-self-improvement.md) synthesizes 6 dimensions of self-improvement (performance profiling, bottleneck detection, auto-optimization, capability expansion, cost optimization, feedback loops) into unified framework. Pattern: (1) Start with executive summary showing system as closed loop, (2) Deep-dive each dimension with code + AWS services, (3) Integration architecture showing how components connect, (4) AWS service mapping with cost breakdown, (5) Production deployment with phased rollout + safety guardrails. 1900+ lines covers Thompson sampling model router, A/B test prompt optimizer, pattern-mining skill generator, idle resource detector, and feedback-driven learning.

content: Research documentation standardization: aws-account-agent series requires Cost Estimate (monthly AWS costs with ROI), Next Steps (navigation to related docs), Summary/Conclusion, and References sections for consistency

name: activity-logging-generators
description: Auto-generate ADRs and runbooks from structured decision/action logs. ADRs capture decision context, alternatives, justification, and rollback plans. Runbooks capture operational procedures, troubleshooting guides, and monitoring from real action execution. Both use template-driven markdown generation with storage abstraction.

content: ADR specification for Chimera architecture: Use standardized template (Status | Context | Alternatives | Decision | Consequences | Evidence | Related Decisions) to formalize architectural decisions extracted from research corpus. Applied template to 18 ADRs across 5 categories: core architecture (6-table DynamoDB, Cedar, Strands, Vercel+SSE bridge, AWS CDK), storage & isolation (monorepo, MicroVM per session, EventBridge nervous system), data & auth (skill adapters, S3+EFS hybrid, self-modifying IaC), operational excellence (Well-Architected framework, CodeCommit+CodePipeline), and tech stack (token bucket rate limiting, Bun+Mise, multi-provider LLM). Critical validation finding: AgentCore sessions ephemeral (no EFS mount support) — workspace storage must use S3 exclusively. Pattern: always validate architectural assumptions against AWS service reality docs.

name: adr-creation-pattern
description: 18 Architecture Decision Records created following standardized template (Status | Context | Alternatives | Decision | Consequences | Evidence | Related Decisions). ADRs document key architectural decisions across 5 categories: core architecture (DynamoDB, Cedar, Strands, Vercel SDK, CDK), storage & isolation (monorepo, MicroVM, EventBridge, skill adapters, S3+EFS), data & auth (self-modifying IaC, AgentCore Memory), operational excellence (Well-Architected framework, CodePipeline), tech stack (token bucket, Bun+Mise, multi-provider LLM, SKILL.md v2). Each ADR includes detailed alternatives analysis with pros/cons, references to research corpus and mulch records, and links to related ADRs. Pattern: extract decisions from research docs + canonical docs + mulch records, score alternatives using Well-Architected pillars, provide evidence citations, cross-reference related ADRs.

name: authoritative-vision-document-structure
description: Comprehensive vision documents follow 12-section pattern covering (1) Identity (system philosophy), (2) Heritage (inspiration sources), (3) Multi-tenant architecture, (4) Universal skills, (5) Multi-platform chat, (6) AWS account intelligence, (7) Infrastructure-as-capability, (8) Autonomous problem-solving, (9) Self-evolution, (10) Structured documentation, (11) Storage architecture, (12) Multi-account management. Cross-reference from README.md with prominent link. Each section includes technical depth (architecture patterns, code examples, decision frameworks) not just marketing copy.

name: research-synthesis-integration-guide
description: Integration guides consolidate multiple research docs (10+ docs, 10K+ lines) into actionable patterns with: (1) Executive summary with go/no-go decision, (2) 5 core integration patterns with CDK TypeScript + Python code examples, (3) Implementation roadmap with phased milestones, (4) Decision matrix for open choices, (5) Known gaps with P0/P1/P2 priorities. Format: concrete code over abstract diagrams, runnable examples over conceptual explanations. Target: implementation teams (leads + builders) who need to start coding immediately.

name: vision-document-research-synthesis
description: Vision documents must be grounded in research findings, not speculation. Pattern: (1) Complete research (OpenClaw 550 lines + AgentCore 1539 lines), (2) Extract operator's vision points from task spec, (3) Structure document around actual capabilities + research-backed claims, (4) Remove marketing fluff and speculative features. Result: 805-line speculative VISION.md replaced with 800-line research-grounded vision covering 10 operator-stated points: AWS account access (not local computer), UTO multi-tenant, skill compatibility, self-evolution, infrastructure as capability, multi-modal, self-reflection, concurrent execution. Evidence: chimera-556c replaced speculative content with synthesis of mx-0a8576 (OpenClaw research) + AgentCore integration guide.

name: multi-builder-merge-cleanup
description: When two builders work on same file (one rewrites, one finalizes), git merge preserves both old and new content. Cleanup builder must explicitly delete duplicate sections. Pattern: (1) git merge other-builder-branch, (2) Read merged file to identify duplicates, (3) Edit to remove old content, preserving new. Common in vision/doc rewrites where writer focuses on content, finalizer focuses on structure.

name: spec-driven-architecture-docs
description: Lead agent synthesizes research into structured mail specs, builder receives content in chunked messages (PART 1, PART 2), writes file in two passes (Write then Edit) to avoid tool size limits. Prevents builder from reading large research files that could stall session. Pattern: complex synthesis by lead -> structured delivery -> direct transcription by builder.

name: vision-document-rewrite-from-research
description: Rewrote VISION.md by synthesizing 2 research docs (OpenClaw/NemoClaw 550 lines + AgentCore/Strands 1539 lines) into 12 operator-stated vision points. Key pattern: operator verbatim priorities as document structure avoids speculative marketing. Core differentiator framing (account access vs computer access) comes from research, not assumption.

content: roadmap-update-methodology: Accurate roadmaps require cross-referencing 4 data sources: (1) actual file counts and LOC via find/wc, (2) seeds issue states via sd list, (3) quality gate results from dispatch/coordinator, (4) git log for recent merges. The dispatch message is the primary source for metrics that require running commands the lead cannot execute (e.g., test results after a rebase).

phase0-completion-documentation-pattern: Phase 0 local development guide covers prerequisites (Bun 1.3+, Node 22+, CDK 2.175+), installation (bun install + npm install in infra/), verification (cdk synth for 11 stacks), TenantAgent L3 construct usage, and troubleshooting. Document structure: prerequisites, installation steps, project structure overview, common commands, construct patterns, testing, cost control tips, and troubleshooting.

All production CloudWatch alarms include RUNBOOK: URL in alarm description for on-call engineers. runbookBaseUrl CDK context prop (e.g., https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/) allows customization. Fallback to inline troubleshooting steps or AWS docs URLs when runbookBaseUrl not configured. Pattern: alarmDescription with 'RUNBOOK: {url}' suffix.

name: alarm-runbooks-structure
description: Alarm-specific runbooks follow template: trigger conditions (CloudWatch query), impact assessment (SEV1-4), quick investigation commands (3-5 bash snippets), resolution options (immediate fix + manual intervention), RCA guidance, prevention measures. Auto-remediation status documented where applicable.

name: disaster-recovery-scenario-structure
description: DR documentation follows scenario-based approach: (1) RTO/RPO table with backup frequency and criticality ratings, (2) Disaster scenarios (regional failure, data corruption, security breach) with step-by-step recovery procedures, (3) Verification commands at each step, (4) Total RTO/RPO for each scenario. PITR (35-day retention) + cross-region replication for critical data (audit logs).

content: production-dr-strategy: RTO/RPO targets documented per component. DynamoDB PITR (5 tables, 5min RPO). S3 versioning (0 RPO). Active-passive cross-region with Route53 failover. DR scenarios: regional failure (25min RTO), data corruption (55min RTO), security breach (6hr RTO). Quarterly PITR drills.

architecture-alignment-review-pattern: Gap analysis documents compare vision/architecture docs against actual implementation with specific code references. Structure: Summary scorecard table (12 vision points × status/coverage/key-gap), detailed point-by-point analysis (what exists, what's missing, verdict percentage), critical gaps ranked by priority, strengths worth preserving. Use for post-implementation validation, pre-deployment readiness checks, or quarterly architecture audits.

chimera-11-stack-architecture: Chimera CDK architecture consists of 11 stacks (not 8): (1) NetworkStack, (2) DataStack, (3) SecurityStack, (4) ObservabilityStack, (5) ApiStack (REST+WebSocket), (6) SkillPipelineStack (7-stage security), (7) ChatStack (ECS), (8) OrchestrationStack (EventBridge+SQS), (9) EvolutionStack (self-improvement), (10) TenantOnboardingStack (Cedar policies), (11) PipelineStack (CI/CD). Stack names follow pattern: Chimera-${envName}-${StackName} (e.g., Chimera-dev-Api). Deployment order defined in infra/bin/chimera.ts with explicit addDependency() calls.

Actual CDK stack count is 11 (not 8 as originally documented). The 11 stacks are: Network, Data, Security, Observability, Api, SkillPipeline, Chat, Orchestration, Evolution, TenantOnboarding, Pipeline. All documentation referencing stack counts must use 11.

ux-review-document-structure: UX review documents follow structure: (1) Executive Summary with status table (✅/❌/⚠️ indicators), (2) Section per UX dimension with Current State + UX Gaps + Recommendations, (3) Mermaid diagrams for flows, (4) Priority matrix (P0/P1/P2) with effort/impact, (5) Implementation roadmap, (6) Success metrics. Documents synthesize research into actionable requirements.

ux-review-gap-analysis-pattern: UX review documents should cover 6 areas systematically: (1) config after deploy, (2) end-to-end setup flow, (3) per-user access model, (4) web portal assessment, (5) chat platform onboarding status, (6) UTO hierarchy gap analysis. Include mermaid diagrams, gap tables with severity, and prioritized recommendations (P0/P1/P2). Key finding: Chimera docs describe features that don't exist in code (portal, channel CLI commands, team/org hierarchy) — gap analysis bridges vision vs implementation.

name: documentation-audit-pattern
description: Docs drift in fast-moving codebases requires periodic audits. Pattern: (1) Run codebase metrics (test counts, LOC, module counts), (2) Compare against README/ROADMAP claims, (3) Update with actual deliverables (checkmark BUILT markers), (4) Rewrite vague future plans as current status, (5) Add concrete metrics tables. Applied to README + ROADMAP: changed Phase 0 85% with 132 tests to reality 85% platform complete, 760 passing tests, 25 AWS tools, full agent runtime. Prevents documentation rot.

name: lead-builder-revision-worktree-isolation
description: Revision builders start from main, not from the previous builder's branch. For revision work, send the previous builder's changes as context in the dispatch mail, or have the revision builder cherry-pick from the previous branch first. Without this, the revision builder makes conflicting changes.

content: agent-identity-documents: SOUL.md (first-person identity, values, decision framework) + AGENTS.md (capability reference with tools, tiers, constraints) injected into agent system prompt for self-awareness. SOUL.md written from agent's perspective ('I am Chimera'), AGENTS.md as reference manual. Both enable deployed agents to understand what they ARE and what they CAN DO.

agent-identity-documents: SOUL.md (agent identity/values/decision-framework, first-person voice) and AGENTS.md (capability reference with all tools/modules/tiers) are injected into agent system prompt on startup. SOUL.md covers identity, 5 values (security-first, reliability-over-speed, cost-awareness, transparency, bounded-autonomy), decision framework (research->plan->build->verify->document with WA 6 pillars), escalation policy, and memory tiers. AGENTS.md catalogs 19 TypeScript AWS tools across 4 tiers, 6 discovery modules, media processing, infra-builder, skill system, orchestration, swarm, evolution engine, well-architected integration, and tiered access (Basic/Advanced/Premium).

documentation-audit-pattern: Comprehensive doc audits verify claims against codebase (e.g., 11-stack claim vs actual infra/lib/ files), identify naming inconsistencies (clawcore vs chimera table prefixes), and prioritize gaps (P0: blockers, P1: high-impact, P2: post-launch). Audit doc template: executive summary, inventory table, detailed findings per doc, gap analysis with effort estimates, implementation verification.

name: documentation-audit-multi-builder-pattern
description: Full docs audit requires (1) filesystem verification of all claims via wc -l ls find, (2) cross-referencing docs vs code structure, (3) separate builder for report writing vs separate builder for targeted fixes. Key findings: LOC claims inflated 31x for Python agent and 26 percent for TS. Non-existent directories referenced in README. Stale table name prefixes in canonical docs. Builder delegation works best with exact imperative fix instructions rather than analytical review requests.

name: aws-tools-audit-pattern
description: Systematic audit of AWS tool implementations identifies three categories: (1) complete tools with full implementations, (2) services with client factory support but no tool files (infrastructure ready), (3) missing services requiring full stack. Documents next-step phases for achieving coverage targets.

name: api-documentation-tooling-research
description: Researched OpenAPI spec generation tools for Express APIs. tsoa (TypeScript decorators) recommended over swagger-jsdoc (JSDoc comments) for type-safe API definitions with runtime validation. AsyncAPI spec handles WebSocket documentation. Serves dual purposes: Swagger UI (internal testing) + Redoc (external docs).

content: api-docs-research-recommendation: swagger-jsdoc recommended for non-invasive OpenAPI spec generation from existing Express routes (25+ endpoints). tsoa is stronger (type safety, runtime validation) but requires full route rewrite. Hybrid approach: start swagger-jsdoc, migrate to tsoa later. AsyncAPI v3 for WebSocket docs. Swagger UI + Redoc for dual rendering.

performance-benchmarking-cost-estimation-methodology: Comprehensive performance and cost model for Chimera at scale (10/100/1000 tenants). Key findings: (1) LLM tokens dominate costs (60-75%), (2) k6 load test targets 1000 concurrent sessions with p95 < 10s, (3) Prompt caching + model routing saves 50-60% on LLM costs, (4) Infrastructure cost per tenant decreases from $40/mo at 10 tenants to $11/mo at 1000 tenants. Documented in docs/research/performance-cost-model.md with k6 test at tests/load/k6-load-test.js and execution plan at docs/research/performance/benchmark-plan.md. CDK infrastructure costs based on actual stack configurations (ECS Fargate 1vCPU/2GB prod, DynamoDB PAY_PER_REQUEST, 7 VPC endpoints, NAT 1-2).

name: performance-cost-model-research
description: Comprehensive performance benchmarking plan and cost estimation for Chimera. Key findings: (1) LLM tokens are 60-75% of total cost, making model routing the highest-impact optimization (100-500x cost difference Nova vs Opus), (2) Fixed infrastructure is ~99/mo dev, ~205/mo prod, (3) Per-session cost excluding LLM is ~0.004, with smart routing total ~0.011/session, (4) k6 recommended over Artillery for load testing, (5) Cost surprise areas at scale include CW custom metrics and Secrets Manager. Output at docs/research/performance-cost-model.md, tests/load/k6-load-test.js.

name: multi-builder-doc-audit-convergence
description: Documentation metric updates across 5+ files required 3 builder iterations to achieve completeness. Key insight: builders tend to update primary metrics (LOC, tool counts) but miss secondary metrics (test fail/error counts, assertion counts, research doc counts). Solution: send ALL exact replacement pairs in the dispatch, include a verification grep command, and explicitly state the total math (860+82+20=962). The final builder succeeded by receiving exhaustive per-file change lists with exact old→new strings.

content: document-tool-choices-early: Tool/package manager conventions (bun vs npm) should be documented prominently early in CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md to prevent confusion. Add 'Development Conventions' or 'Development Setup' section before workflow details. Prevents wrong command usage and explains WHY choices were made (e.g., bunx for devDeps).

docs-npm-npx-consistency: Documentation must use 'bun install' not 'npm install', and 'bunx cdk' not 'npx cdk' or bare 'cdk' commands. CDK is a devDependency, not a global install. This applies to README, quickstart guides, deployment guides, and all operational docs.

name: feature-first-documentation-audit
description: Documentation audits should map features → docs (not just files → existence). Feature-by-feature coverage matrix with quality scores reveals implementation-documentation misalignment that file-centric audits miss. Example: 70% feature coverage vs. 90% file existence accuracy.

content: dual-guide-directories: Chimera has two guide directories: docs/guide/ (5 core guides: architecture, deployment, multi-tenant, quickstart, skills) and docs/guides/ (4 operational guides: cicd-pipeline, disaster-recovery, local-development, uto-setup-guide). Previous audits only covered docs/guides/. Both must be included in all documentation audits.

name: feature-first-doc-audit
description: Feature-first documentation audits (map each implemented feature to its docs) reveal more gaps than file-first audits (check each doc for accuracy). Previous file-first audit rated 90%, feature-first re-audit found 70% by discovering undocumented features like swarm orchestration (45%), API reference (60%), and CLI commands (65%).

name: multi-part-mail-doc-assembly
description: Lead agent sends research content in numbered parts via mail (Part 1-4), builder agent waits for all parts, then assembles complete document with proper markdown formatting. Pattern enables large content transfer without single-message limits. Used for chimera-6e32 Manus research doc.

name: scout-first-dispatch-pattern
description: Lead spawns scout agents to read files, scouts send exact change specs via mail to builders. Builders apply changes WITHOUT re-reading codebase. Prevents context exhaustion in multi-agent workflows.

name: canonical-architecture-reference-doc
description: Single consolidated ARCHITECTURE.md that serves as system entry point - includes system overview, architecture diagrams (mermaid), stack map with dependencies, package map, ADR index, communication patterns, and data flows. Supersedes scattered architecture docs.

content: architecture-reference-doc-sections: ARCHITECTURE.md canonical reference follows 8-section structure: System Overview (1 para), Architecture Diagram (mermaid flowchart TB), Deployment Flow (mermaid LR + pipeline steps), Stack Map (table + dependency graph), Package Map (table + text dependency graph), ADR Index (categorized tables), Communication Patterns (SSE, EventBridge, SQS, Memory tiers), Data Flow (mermaid sequence diagram + text flows).

name: adr-documentation-pattern
description: ADRs capture production-validated decisions with Status/Context/Decision/Alternatives/Consequences/Evidence sections. Follow ADR-001 template format. Document WHY decisions were made, not just WHAT was chosen. Include Evidence section linking to implementation files and mulch records.

content: adr-creation-pattern: ADRs 019-023 document runtime and deployment decisions. Template: YAML frontmatter (title, status, date, decision_makers) + sections (Status, Context, Decision, Alternatives with Pros/Cons/Verdict, Consequences Positive/Negative/Risks, Evidence with mulch records, Related Decisions, References). Each ADR references specific mulch records as evidence. README index updated with section grouping and metadata.

adr-audit-findings-2026-03-24: Of 18 ADRs, 7 are partially outdated. Most significant: ADR-004 claims Vercel Chat SDK but actual is Hono + Vercel DSP protocol. ADR-005 claims 8 stacks but actual is 11. ADR-006 has wrong package directory listing. ADR-002/011/017 describe aspirational features (Cedar, self-modifying IaC, LiteLLM) not yet implemented.

name: adr-aspirational-vs-implemented
description: ADRs written during architecture phase may describe aspirational designs not yet implemented. Cedar (ADR-002), DynamoDB-driven CDK (ADR-011), and LiteLLM gateway (ADR-017) are documented as accepted but have no implementation code. ADRs should distinguish decided from implemented status.

content: session-retrospective-format: Session retrospectives follow structured format with accomplishments, patterns, challenges, metrics, and future work sections

name: codebase-map-navigation
description: AGENTS.md includes codebase map section after Development Setup for quick navigation and project structure understanding

content: session-retrospective-template: Session retrospective docs should cover: Executive Summary, Key Accomplishments (with agent/task references), Best Practices Codified (grouped by domain), Codebase Structure Insights, Patterns Applied, Challenges Overcome, Metrics, Future Work, Retrospective Learnings (what went well / what to improve / action items), and Mulch Records.

custom-domain-guide-structure: Custom domain docs cover 3 endpoint types (CloudFront chat, REST API, WebSocket) across 4 DNS providers (Route53, Cloudflare x3 options, Namecheap, GoDaddy) with SES/ACM/APIGW sections. Critical: ACM certs for CloudFront must be us-east-1; Cloudflare endpoint records must be DNS-only (grey cloud). chimera.toml [domain] section is proposed/new - not yet in CDK.

architecture-alignment-review-pattern: Gap analysis documents cross-reference session directives, ROADMAP.md, codebase audit (WELL_ARCHITECTED_ANALYSIS), and research corpus to produce a prioritized backlog with P0-P3 items and open-question resolution guidance. Sources: session-40-user-directives.md, ROADMAP.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, WELL_ARCHITECTED_ANALYSIS.md, enhancement/00-Gap-Analysis-Report.md

ADR tier naming canonical: TenantTier is 'basic|advanced|enterprise|dedicated' from @chimera/shared. Aliases 'pro' and 'premium' are legacy; forbidden in new code. Memory strategy tiers are a separate internal concept. Cedar policies must cover all 4 tiers.

ADR batch pattern: L3 constructs (ADR-026) encode project-specific DynamoDB/S3/Lambda/SQS defaults + CDK Aspects for enforcement; React+Vite+shadcn (ADR-027) chosen over Next.js for authenticated SPA dashboards; Amplify Gen2 connects to existing CDK-managed Cognito without Amplify backend deployment (ADR-028)

adr-format-spec-content: When a builder receives a dispatch mail with FILE content between === markers, that content MUST be written exactly as specified — the spec overrides any research-based content the builder may have independently prepared.

name: adr-batch-writing-via-mail
description: When lead cannot write files, send full ADR content to builders via ov mail dispatch body. Builders copy content exactly. 3 builders for 7 ADRs (2-3 each) is optimal batch size — enough parallelism without excessive spawn overhead.

content: Spec files in docs/specs/ follow standard frontmatter: title, status, references (ADR list), priority (P0/P1/P2), estimated_effort (S/M/L/XL). Sections: Objective, Background, Detailed Changes, Acceptance Criteria, Test Requirements, Dependencies, Risk Assessment.

name: spec-writing-lead-builder-pattern
description: Lead agents cannot write files directly due to hooks. For spec-writing tasks, spawn a single builder and send spec content via ov mail (one mail per spec or split large specs into parts). Builder writes files verbatim and commits. Lead self-verifies by reading builder worktree diff. Mail body length limit requires splitting large content across multiple messages.

content: api-stack-mock-integration-comment-pattern: All MockIntegration usages in api-stack.ts must include INTENTIONAL comment block explaining: (1) this is placeholder until PlatformRuntimeStack wires real Lambda handlers, (2) chat-gateway Hono on ECS Fargate handles real traffic, (3) reference to ADR-024. Three locations: addPlaceholderMethod private method, webhook routes loop, OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

docs-sync-pattern: When updating stale docs across README/ROADMAP/ADR index, run actual count commands (find for test files, ls for tool dirs, ls for module dirs) before editing — do not trust prior snapshots. bun test output gives current pass/fail counts.

first-deployment-runbook: CLI command config usage — init writes [aws]+[workspace], deploy reads both+writes [deployment], endpoints reads [aws]+[workspace]+writes [endpoints], doctor reads [aws]+[endpoints], login reads [endpoints]+[auth], chat reads [endpoints] via api-client, status reads [aws]+[workspace]
